ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean white bee hoon. FOODON_03315671 (rice vermicelli dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Rice noodles) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for white bee hoon as a prepared Singaporean dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for white bee hoon or Singaporean rice vermicelli dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large family-style platter weighing approximately 1200 g, intended to serve 4 to 6 people, with each individual portion estimated at approximately 200–300 g.

nutrient_source.title: White Bee Hoon (Fried) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists fried bee hoon with seafood as the closest match. Values are adapted from HPB's fried bee hoon (seafood) entry. The 'white' preparation style (no dark soy sauce, lighter seasoning, lard-enriched broth) is characteristic of this dish; fat and sodium values are adjusted upward slightly to reflect the richer lard-based broth typical of white bee hoon. Per-100g values are estimated from the HPB fried bee hoon (seafood) baseline with reference to USDA SR Legacy data for individual components (rice vermicelli, prawns, squid, egg).

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean white bee hoon. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low category). The dish is predominantly rice vermicelli with seafood and broth; the protein and fat content from seafood and lard moderately lower the effective GI relative to plain vermicelli. GI 53 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 17.5 × 250) / 100 / 100 = approximately 23.2 for a 250 g individual portion; reported here as 18.6 for a 200 g reference portion: (53 × 17.5 × 200) / 100 / 100 = 18.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840–1260 mg per individual serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, as a single serving can contribute 35–50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from rice vermicelli with low GI (~53)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index of rice vermicelli (GI ~53) produces a more gradual postprandial glucose rise compared to wheat noodles or white rice; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150–200 g per meal and monitor total carbohydrate intake, as a 200 g serving provides approximately 35 g of carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lard and cholesterol from seafood and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The use of lard in traditional white bee hoon preparation contributes saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) and the seafood and egg components add dietary cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request preparation with vegetable oil substituted for lard where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature White Bee Hoon (Large) is a quintessential Singaporean Chinese seafood noodle dish featuring thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon) cooked in a rich, milky-white pork or chicken stock enriched with lard, giving the broth its characteristic opaque white colour and silky mouthfeel. The large portion is designed to serve 4 to 6 people as a shared family-style meal. The dish is distinguished from other bee hoon preparations by the absence of dark soy sauce, resulting in a pale, clean-flavoured broth seasoned with fish sauce, light soy sauce, and white pepper. Key ingredients include fresh prawns, squid, clams, egg, garlic, ginger, and spring onion. The rice vermicelli absorbs the savoury broth and remains soft yet slightly springy in texture. The dish is associated with the Teochew and Hokkien culinary traditions of Singapore and is a popular zi char (cooked-to-order) restaurant staple. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from seafood (~7.8 g per 100 g), and a relatively low glycemic index (~53) due to the rice vermicelli base. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains crustacean, mollusc, egg, and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal dietary requirements due to the use of lard.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for 'white bee hoon' as a distinct dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore's fried bee hoon (seafood) entry with component-level cross-referencing against USDA SR Legacy data for rice vermicelli, prawns, squid, and egg. The adaptation is defensible but introduces estimation uncertainty, particularly for fat and sodium which are sensitive to preparation style and lard usage. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in rice vermicelli and seafood-based dishes per HPB and USDA sources. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (boiled rice vermicelli); no direct GI measurement for white bee hoon exists in the published literature. 6. The large (L) portion is priced at SGD 26.00 and served by The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon) at The Riverwalk, Singapore. 7. The dish is not halal-certified due to the traditional use of lard; individual restaurants may offer lard-free versions. 8. Allergens include crustaceans, molluscs, eggs, and soy. 9. The food_id GD003557 is a field survey-sourced seed record not present in the FD series food index.
